expectations.text: Q1 verdict reflects that this exceeded targets — the retro captures success, not just problems
expectations.evidence: Q1 verdict is 'SOLVED' with summary: 'The new onboarding flow exceeded its adoption target by 12 percentage points and meaningfully reduced support burden.' Explicitly states 'This is a genuine success.'

expectations.text: Q2 delivery assessment shows all-green metrics (on time, spec quality, scope maintained) — documenting what went right is what makes success repeatable
expectations.evidence: All five Q2 items marked [PASS]. Includes explicit documentation of WHY it went well: 'Spec was clear enough that engineering had no ambiguity questions', 'Scope was locked — no mid-sprint additions', 'team confidence is a leading indicator worth paying attention to.'

expectations.text: Q4 still produces at least one process observation — even successful features have learnings; the retro must not skip this section just because things went well
expectations.evidence: Three process improvements documented: (1) formalize what made this spec work, (2) understand and document why scope held, (3) start tracking team confidence as a leading indicator. All are forward-looking improvements, not criticisms.

expectations.text: The retro does NOT only document what went wrong — per the NEVER rules, a retro that skips what went well fails to protect what works
expectations.evidence: The retro is overwhelmingly focused on what went well and how to replicate it. Q1 celebrates the success, Q2 documents the clean delivery, Q4 extracts replicable patterns. This is a success-documenting retro.
